The parameter obj to the print_num() function is value parameter. It will be a new object and is a copy of argument. That is the reason, it is created  using copy ctor.

question: What will be the output of the following program?
options: Program crashes because of shallow copy
explanation:
The class does not have a copy ctor. So compiler provides a copy ctor which does shallow copy.

When obj2 is copy created, both obj1 and obj2 point to same dynamic memory. When if statement ends, obj2 is destroyed and its destructor is called. Destructor releases memory. 

Next when obj3 is created, it tries to copy contents pointed by pointer. But the memory pointed by pointer is released. Hence program crashes.

question: Why is virtual inheritance used?
options: To avoid occurance dreaded diamond problem
explanation:
Virtual inheritance is used to avoid dreaded diamond problem when using multiple and multilevel.

When a class is included more than once due to multiple inheritance, there will be an ambiguity in accessing member of this class.

Virtual inheritance ensures that only one copy of this class is included regardless of number of paths to the base class.

explanation:
An object of abstract class can not be created. So we can not create object of A class.

A class which inherits an abstract class becomes abstract UNLESS it defines the pure virtual function. B does not define someFunction() function. So we can not create an object of class B too.

But we can create a pointer to an abstract class with the assumption that it would be pointing to one of non-abstract derived classes. So line 3 is correct

question: What is the output of the following program?

A constructor B constructor A destructor
explanation:
A dervied class constructor always calls base class constructor and  derived class destructor always calls base class destructor. 

But here, objptr is a A pointer - that is base class pointer. So when delete objptr is called, it calls only base class destructor. 

To overcome this problem, destructor should be made virtual.

We should not use backslash as separator, instead use / even in Windows systems.

As D class inherits two sub-objects of A class - one through B and another through A, it inherits all the members of A class twice. Hence accessing A members throws an ambiguity error. This is called dreaded diamond problem. The inheritance diagram resembles a diamond.

To avoid such errors we should use virtual inheritance. 

Note: We can still access A members using ::
D.A::print();
